data.title: Zeta: The AI Play Wall Street Misses
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last Zeta Global Holdings Corp. (NYSE:ZETA) coverage, the stock is up 10.5% slightly lagging the S&P 500's 11.3% return. Previously, the focus was on Zeta's strong 38% revenue growth, expanding client base, and accelerating AI investments as the basis of its $2.1 billion CY28 topline target.

That bullish thesis remains intact, but now the thesis is solidified with the launch of Zeta's AI Agent Studio, deeper client wallet share, and vertical expansion. However, dilution from stock-based compensation (SBC) tempers the near-term upside. Still, with sector-beating growth and valuation below the sector average, Zeta is a pick for AI bulls in marketing tech.

Zeta Eyes $2 Billion Revenue by FY28 on 40%+ AI Adoption Growth

Zeta Global's strength lies in its AI Agent Studio and agentic workflows that deliver competitive return on investment (ROI) to marketers on omnichannel campaigns. These campaigns are based on precision as they leverage AI to target, personalize, measure, and optimize across owned and paid media. This capability is creating a moat for Zeta as currently marketing budgets face intense scrutiny and its products hold tangible value to clients.

For instance, a large telecommunications client hit a 53% reduction in customer acquisition cost (CAC) compared to its benchmark. As a result, that led to a two-year agreement that more than doubled its annual investment with Zeta. Similarly, a major insurance provider saw a 37% lower CAC versus its FY24 target which prompted a projected 50% increase in spending for FY25. In another instance, a financial institution beat its CAC target by 14% resulting in a 20% spend increase following a 150% jump in FY24.

These outcomes are validated through third-party attribution methods, in short, Zeta holds the capability to deliver repeatable, scalable results, boosting client expansion. The company's net revenue retention rate has been consistently at 111% or higher since its 2021 IPO and will continue to be at this trend by FY28.

Zeta's AI Agent Studio, launched in Q1-FY25 boosts its value proposition through AI tools that support marketers to create and deploy custom AI agents for complex tasks. Agentic workflows push the coordination of multiple AI agents on processes like audience segmentation, strategy development, and cross-channel activation, reducing time-to-execution to boost ROI.

For instance, a client used a multi-agent workflow to identify high-value audiences and activate campaigns across multiple channels with a single prompt (a big efficiency gain). Clients adopting these AI agents are growing consumption revenue at a rate exceeding 40% YoY that indicates the platform's transformative potential.

Zeta's focus on lower-funnel conversion metrics that are less discretionary than top-of-funnel brand awareness spend improves its growth stability. This can be observed during past issues like the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2022 tech reset when Zeta grew revenue by 20% and 29%.

In Q1-FY25, Zeta had revenue of $264 million with a 36% YoY increase and adjusted EBITDA of $46.7 million (+53% YoY) both surpassing guidance. These results point to the Zeta platform's capability to derive high growth even amidst macro uncertainty. In Q1-FY25, Zeta expanded its scaled client count by 19% YoY to 548 and super-scaled client count by 10% YoY to 159. Super-scaled client ARPU rising 23% to $1.4 million.

The platform's capability to derive growth across six of its top 10 verticals, each growing over 20% YoY on a trailing 12-month basis. So there is a broad applicability of its products. Zeta's strategic focus on large enterprises that account for over 90% of its revenue from clients with more than one year of tenure further stabilizes its topline growth.

The company's deals with agencies, including both independent agencies and large holding companies also push the topline growth potential. In Q1-FY25, Zeta platformed two new independent agencies and saw solid growth with holding companies. For instance, an independent agency used Zeta's retail audience intelligence to secure a new client, creating a win-win scenario.

Looking forward, Zeta's 2028 plan is targeting over $2 billion in revenue and Zeta's current valuation metrics are favorable. For instance, its forward P/S of 2.9x is ~4% below the sector median of 3.1x, and its forward EV/EBITDA of 13.2x is 13% below the sector median of 15x, despite superior growth (26% forward revenue growth vs. 7.09% sector median and 35.17% forward EBITDA growth vs. 9.22% sector median).

Based on the P/S analysis, if Zeta hit a forward P/S of 3.1 by FY28, in line with the sector median (through growth and market share gains), the FY28 revenue at $2 billion (per Zeta's 2028 plan) yields a target market cap of $6.2 billion. This is a ~70% upside from the current market cap of $3.65 billion.

3-4% Dilution in 2026 to Sustain 40%+ AI Topline Growth

Zeta's high SBC and resulting share dilution is a major downside to the stock price. However, it supports growth by enabling the company to attract and retain critical talent. In Q1 Zeta projected $190 million in SBC for FY25 with a slight reduction from $195 million in FY24. But still massive compared to its $264 million quarterly revenue.

This high SBC will lead to a core share count dilution of 4% to 6% in FY25 down from 15% in FY24 that included 8% from normal equity grants. While this dilution pressures the stock's market value, reflected in Zeta's elevated price-to-book ratio of 5.3x, 54% above the sector median of 3.51.

The reason behind such a high SBC is to support Zeta's topline growth by securing top-tier engineering and AI specialists. Against AI giants like Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), retaining these professionals is needed for maintaining Zeta's tech lead in solutions like AI Agent Studio and agentic workflows and revenue growth.

In short, there is a direct link between talent investment and business outcomes. So, Zeta's capability to be a differentiator in the marketing tech amid competitors with legacy systems that face issues due to technical debt (like latency in AI processing outside the core marketing cloud) depends on competitive equity packages to AI architects and engineers. The company is making heavy investments in AI through the continued allocation of considerable resources to research and development, but CapEx as a percentage of revenue is declining due to rapid revenue growth.

However, the high SBC and dilution present real issues in terms of value erosion and elevated valuation metrics compared to historical averages. To address these concerns, Zeta has taken proactive steps like reducing FY25 SBC by $5 million and further dilution reductions to 3% to 4% in FY26. Additionally, top executives, including Steinberg, CFO Chris Greiner, and Steve Gerber, have opted out of FY25 equity grants. Zeta's share repurchase program retired 1.6 million shares for $25 million in Q1-FY25 and an additional 1.8 million for $21 million by April 25, minimizing dilution and boosting per-share value.

Overall, despite these moves, the high SBC remains a double-edged sword. It supports growth through tech leads and derives client expansion, but it also caps valuation multiples and stock price appreciation. The company's moves to balance talent investment with dilution control can sustain its topline growth and improve its valuation profile. As Zeta progresses toward its FY28 targets of over $2 billion in revenue and a 25% adjusted EBITDA margin, its lead in leveraging AI will boost its stock value in the long term.

Takeaway

Zeta Global offers a rare upside: 40%+ AI-fueled growth, sticky client wins, and sector-beating ROI, all at a discount to peers. Despite dilution risks, disciplined cost controls and executive restraint are boosting per-share value. For AI-driven marketers, Zeta's underpriced trajectory toward $2 billion in revenue by FY28 is a compelling long-term bet.

data.title: Medicus Pharma: High-Risk But Higher Reward For Their Potential Breakthrough Skin Cancer Treatment
data.description:
Company Background

Medicus Pharma Ltd. (NASDAQ:MDCX) describes itself as ‘a biotech/life sciences company focused on accelerating the clinical development programs of novel and disruptive therapeutic assets’. The company came about in October 2023 as the result of a reverse takeover between the publicly-traded shell company Interactive Capital Partners Corporation, based in Canada, and SkinJect, a University of Pittsburgh spin-off based in Pennsylvania. Medicus remains officially domiciled in Toronto but is administratively based in Conshohocken, PA (just outside of Philadelphia). The stock was initially traded only on the TSX-V exchange, but IPO’d on the NASDAQ in November 2024, raising $4 million in the process. In April 2025, the company announced it was stepping away from the TSX-V exchange.

As part of the original transaction, the founder of SkinJect, Dr. Raza Bokhari, stepped in as CEO and Chairman of the Board to lead the new entity. SkinJect became, in effect, a subsidiary of Medicus but, at this point in the company’s story, its product of the same name remains its key focus and opportunity for near-term revenue generation for Medicus.

Through SkinJect, Medicus is attempting to bring to market its innovative basal cell carcinoma (non-melanoma skin cancer) solution, which is essentially a patch put on the skin that delivers a chemotherapy drug through fully dissolvable microneedles. It is novel and potentially transformative for the industry.

If approved, SkinJect could represent a $2 billion opportunity for the company according to the CEO. With a current market cap of just $53 million, this is one of those high risk, high reward opportunities that, really – like so many – could go one way or the other.

It also recently acquired another company (Antev), which I’ll discuss below, that offers the opportunity to derisk an investment here, as their takeover target had two pipeline treatments in the works that could also offer transformative solutions in both acute urinary retention (ACRr) and cardiovascular high-risk prostate cancer. As such, if even just one of these treatments received FDA approval, the potential bonanza for investors could be considerable. Such is the confidence that Jason Kolbert of D. Boral Capital and Jason McCarthy of Maxim Group have in the company’s prospects, the analysts have assigned a $27 and $20 target price, respectively, on the stock. With shares trading at $3.38 as of July 3, this represents a 600% upside at a mean projected value of $23.50.

In the remainder of this article, I’ll expand upon its treatment pipeline and offer my take on its chances of success – based on my modest knowledge around this biotech space, from what information I can gather – before discussing its balance sheet and assessing its risk/reward profile at current prices.

Interesting Prospects for SkinJect

What makes Medicus interesting as an early-stage, pre-revenue biotech start-up is its strong history of financial support, much of which has come in the form of mixed insider/institutional backing. Currently, as far as I can tell, approximately 40% of the company’s outstanding shares are owned by either company insiders or institutions also owned or operated by company insiders.

The largest institutional backer, Velocity Fund Partners, was created in 2016 by two CEOs with different but complementary backgrounds in healthcare and life sciences. They began their investment in SkinJect in 2016, and since then one of the two, Kenneth Melani, has acquired shares directly. The near 40% combined insider and institutional ownership in the company is a positive. Skin in the game helps ensure management, board members and shareholders stay aligned in operational directives, focused on creating value.

The board includes a mix of business and medical leaders, which I consider an appropriate mix considering the mammoth medical and entrepreneurial undertaking that the company is working through. At present, their most interesting prospect is SkinJect, which was invented by Dr. Louis Falo, Chairman of the dermatology department at the University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine, who remains involved in the clinical development of his product and a minority stakeholder in Medicus, and Dr. O. Burak Ozdoganlar, a professor in Mechanical Engineering and Materials Science and Engineering at Carnegie Mellon University, who remains a technical advisor.

Their proprietary patch is comprised of ‘a thumb-sized array of dissolvable microneedles that delivers a chemotherapeutic agent to kill an existing skin cancer’, according to a recent news report. The microneedles deliver the chemotherapeutic agent Doxorubicin transdermally at the site of the skin lesion. It triggers an immune response to eradicate the tumor cells.

According to the Skin Cancer Foundation, basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common form of non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) , with approximately 3.6 million patients diagnosed annually in the US alone. The majority (between 70 and 80%) of all skin cancers are of the basal cell variety, and while this tends to have very high survival rates, if left untreated it could spread to other areas and become fatal over time. Indeed, research published in the journal JAMA Oncology found that in 2017 there were 7.7 million cases of NMSC globally of which 5.9 million were BCC, and 65,000 deaths worldwide due to NMSC. Even more likely, and at the very least, the lesions caused by the disease can leave permanent disfigurement.

SkinJect is described as a ‘cost-effective’ and ‘simple treatment for patients’ – it is painless and easy to administer – and provides an alternative to the present standard of care, Mohs surgery, which involves removing basal cells (and some healthy cells around it) layer by layer with a scalpel. It can also leave lasting scars and physical disfigurement. Alternatively, SkinJect is administered during three separate thirty-minute appointments in a doctor’s office, and early indications suggest that it provides much better results aesthetically (see above).

SkinJect offers a viable alternative to Mohs surgery, with specific benefits related to safety, convenience (ease of use), time to treatment, and cost, but with lower efficacy rates. The practical advantages of the SkinJect treatment are such that, if found to be safe and effective, it could represent a game changer in the treatment of this very common form of skin cancer. You can imagine millions of patients opting for a simple, cheaper, non-invasive patch on their arm instead of surgery in the first instance. Therefore, it could easily become the first-choice option, with patients only moving on to surgery if the results are ineffective.

So, while the CEO sees this as a $2 billion a year market that Medicus could take share in, I actually believe it could be much larger. The market size for treating BCC is estimated to be around $15-20 billion annually, and while SkinJect will be primarily aimed at those who are either not suitable or would prefer not to have surgery, in time – again if it is proven to work as effectively and safely as hoped – I foresee this treatment option potentially becoming the standard practice. Of course, it should be noted that this is a competitive space, but SkinJect’s unique approach makes it stand out. Its patents cover worldwide distribution and last until 2035, which gives it several years head-start in this product line.

Medicus believes that SkinJect could also become a treatment option for other forms of skin cancer, specifically squamous cell carcinoma and actinic keratosis, thereby increasing the market size still further. In an article in the Philadelphia Business Journal last year, Bokhari called the experimental treatment ‘a special asset’, and ‘a cure for the cancer’.

Bokhari’s mix of physician and business expertise makes him an interesting and compelling leader in this space. He was previously CEO and executive chairman of FSD Pharma, which rebranded last year as Quantum BioPharma (QNTM). While he successfully refocused FSD Pharma during his tenure from medicinal cannabis to clinical-stage pharmaceutical research and development, and brought them to a nearly $100 million IPO in 2020, his spell at the company’s helm ended with termination. Bokhari responded by filing a wrongful dismissal lawsuit, which he eventually lost resulting in a settlement paid by Bokhari to Quantum of $2.35 million in damages and other legal fees.

While this information is not necessarily relevant to his work at Medicus, it is particularly instructive that several of his former employees followed Bokhari to Medicus in spite of all this. This included the current CMO Dr. Ed Brennan who worked alongside Bokhari at FSD, and Carolyn Bonner, who worked as CEO of Parkway Clinical Laboratories, a toxicology testing and monitoring facility, which Bokhari chaired the board for. I view this as a positive endorsement of the man. He was also a previous recipient of Philadelphia Business Journal's "40 under 40" award, and recently, after a gift of $1 million made to his alma mater, Temple University, their ‘Fox Business School named the Innovation & Entrepreneurship Institute Suite in his honor’, so says the 2024 10-K.

While all of this is positive, clearly, for investors to reap the benefits, the product must be approved and successfully commercialized, and there are many hurdles that Medicus must navigate on this journey that could take several years. In this video recorded in May, Bokhari suggested ‘sometime in 2027’ to reach commercial availability, but I suspect it will take a bit longer to reach the stage of revenue generation. Indeed, in their latest corporate presentation, the company stated its plans to file for FDA approval in Q2 2027, which means commercialization and then subsequent revenue generation could be another 12 months from then. The year 2028 is more likely for revenue generation.

For investors, then, patience is key here. However, fundamentally, the most important question is: does SkinJect actually work? That is what the company is trying to prove right now.

SkinJect received Investigational New Drug approval from the FDA to study the patch as a treatment for BCC in 2018. In March 2021, the Phase 1 trial to test for safety and tolerability met the primary goal in both these regards. Six of the 13 participants had a complete response, defined as the complete disappearance of BCC histologically by the time of their final visit. In January 2024, Medicus received approval to begin enrollment for a Phase 2 proof-of-concept study and started recruiting up to 60 patients in August. Interim results published in March showed 60% clinical clearance, and by the following month, the trial population was increased from 60 to 90 participants. This is a positive sign, alongside the initiation of a further trial in UAE and another forthcoming in Europe. Eventual approval in the latter would increase the TAM for SkinJect by a significant margin; data from Europe suggests around 700,000 diagnosed cases of BCC each year.

Results from these trials, especially the Phase 2 trial in the US (SKNJCT-003), are of vital importance, and come with the hope of advancing the research program to a larger phase 3 trial involving 200-400 patients. This data read-out, should it start on time, is expected in Q2 2026, and the results will be of vital importance, giving a strong indication, or otherwise, of future approval in 2027/28. But again, we’re quite a long way off from there, and I suspect investors will have plenty of time to pick up shares before they represent the share price corresponding to the potential future revenue bonanza they have anticipated.

SkinJect for Animals

Meanwhile, outside of the larger market of skin cancer treatments for humans, Medicus has made some progress from a veterinary perspective as well, an untapped market as far as carcinoma treatments is concerned. In December, SkinJect received MUMS designation – meaning Minor Use in Major Species – which means it can be tested on animals, and specifically in this case, to treat external squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) in horses. MUMS status is similar to the Orphan Drug status for humans. It incentivizes companies to test and bring to market treatments that have limited population sizes, by providing tax credits and extended exclusivity following approval.

SCC presents as a mucocutaneous skin tumor in horses, mainly affecting adult white or white-coated horses of Appaloosa, Belgian, American Paint, and Pinto breeds. Tumors are often seen around the eyes, lips, nose, anus, and external genitalia. Of the 7 million or so horses in the US, SCC will present itself in about 2-3% of them (roughly 175,000 horses per year). According to the press release, current treatments include surgery to remove the largest tumor mass, cryotherapy of the smaller mass, then a local injection of a chemotherapy drug. In other cases, a chemotherapy drop or cream is administered, or horses are put on an oral drug called piroxicam (PFE) that ‘may slow the return of the cancerous cells’.

As such, with standard treatment options seeming to be quite complicated and burdensome, I suspect a decent opportunity here for Medicus. Bokhari suggested a market in the range of $250 million, which seems a reasonable estimate to me, though perhaps a little high. Indeed, if the price remains $1000 per treatment, which it the price it is looking to be sold for humans, then multiplied by 175,000 a year equals $175 million. In any case, in early June, Medicus submitted a ‘comprehensive product development plan’ to the FDA, with an intention to be first to market with the drug approved and commercially available in 2026.

With SCC also common in cats and dogs and a host of other pets and livestock, the market for this product, in a non-human population alone, could be significant, and far more than $250 million. For shareholders, it is another development that derisks an investment here.

Recent Acquisition – Antev

On June 30, Medicus announced it has finalized the deal to acquire Antev, a UK-based clinical-stage drug development company. The price paid was a 17% equity stake in MDCX (2,666,600 shares), subject to a lock-up period, plus up to $65 million extra if their treatments are advanced to approval. The deal is expected to close by the end of the August. Most attractive to Medicus were their two pipeline treatments involving Teverelix.

Teverelix is a GnRH antagonist (hormone therapy) that is being tested to treat prostate cancer for men with a history of cardiovascular (CV) disease, that is, they are at high risk of getting CV disease. This is a niche area but an important one. Hormone therapies for prostate cancer – what are called ADTs or androgen deprivation treatments – often increase the risk of CV complications such as heart attacks, strokes, and other CV-related problems, according to published academic research from 2022. In part, this is because ADTs impact blood pressure, cholesterol levels, and other CV-related risk factors. The researchers found that, of ‘men treated with hormone therapy compared to those who were not’, there was a 42% higher risk of dying from a stroke and a 70% higher risk of dying from coronary heart disease. As such, Teverelix could represent a best-in-class hormone therapy for this special indication or niche. For this, they anticipate a TAM of $4 billion annually.

Secondly, Teverelix is also indicated for treatment of Acute Urinary Retention ((AURr)) episodes for men with an enlarged prostate, what is called Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia. AURr symptoms include an inability to urinate, or fully empty the bladder, and abdominal pain. Current treatment options include catheterization as an immediate response, followed by medications that relax the prostate muscles like alpha-adrenergic blockers Flomax or Uroxatral, manufactured by Sanofi (SNY). For this indication, Antev is currently undergoing a phase 2 study, and management believes this could be the first in class therapy with a $2 billion TAM annually.

The CEO implied a synergy when commenting that the Antev deal provided, alongside SkinJect, another ‘asset that fulfils an unmet medical need, and is novel and disruptive in nature’, but I have some reservations about what connects these two pipelines with their ‘flagship’ SkinJect product—therefore, I question the synergy potential. However, I like the acquisition in terms of what it represents, that is, a derisking of the investment. Instead of having all their eggs in one basket, they get to increase their chances of successful commercialization by adding further treatments, at the cost of 17% of their company (and shareholder equity). With a potential $6 billion market opportunity combined for both indications (and possibly more in the future), the dilutive impact created by this acquisition is relatively modest at this point and will be more than offset if – and it remains a big if – one of these two pipelines leads to the successful commercialization of a treatment.

Financials

Medicus is a start-up biotech company with strong potential but must endure several quarters of no revenue forthcoming. In order for it to survive, let alone prosper, the company needs to stay in the black for at least two years in my estimation. Given the speed of trials and regulatory approval, I feel a late-2026 FDA approval for SkinJect in horses is possible, but more likely 2027. If the approval process gets sped up due to it receiving fast-track designation, this would help enormously, but there is no guarantee of this. Moreover, with the new acquisition it is having to fund 2 major pipelines in two separate locations (one in PA, the other in the UK), meaning little opportunity for synergistic cost-cutting (sharing lab space, human or physical resources etc.).

According to the latest 10-Q, filed in May, Medicus incurred a net loss of just over $5.1 million in the previous quarter ($0.42 per share), and an accumulated net loss of around $34 million. Of this quarterly loss, about 60% was from general and administrative costs, of which $465,000 was from business development and investor relations expenses (essentially, marketing). This seems excessive to me, given they are years from a saleable product. Moreover, as they creep closer and closer to commercialization, these expenses will only increase as they marketing efforts accelerate and the need for a sales team increases. The other $2 million from R&D costs is less of a concern to me, though I’d like to see R&D continue to represent a larger proportion of their operating expenses going forward. That said, this figure is also likely to increase noticeably as the company moves into a phase 3 trial for SkinJect that will aim for 200-400 patients across multiple sites, both in the US and abroad.

The company also recorded a $3.94 million operating cash burn for the quarter. They kept themselves afloat through $3.76 million of financing, mainly the proceeds of selling stock and warrants.

The share count increased from 8 million to 13.4 million (representing a little over 67% dilution from last year), which is very significant, but the CEO claimed in this video that the company is ‘well situated for the remainder of this year and beyond to advance our development programs without any other major dilution capital infusion.’ I am not so sure it won’t seek more cash, but perhaps these secondary offerings will be ‘minor’ in nature, but what this means is entirely subjective. In my estimation, investors should brace for massive dilution going forward. If they are currently burning through $4 million a quarter, they’ll need $32 million to cover them for another two years, and that is if their quarterly expenses don’t increase, which I suspect they will. Indeed, they’ll probably need closer to $50 million, which is their current market cap. To put it another way, the share price will have to double in price to compensate for 100% added dilution. Thus, in my estimation it is likely the share price will continue to be impacted by repeated news of stock offerings and dilution, as it has been in the past.

What is clear, however, is that the company is very good at raising capital. This is great, because they’ll need lots of it. Indeed, with cash and equivalents of $3.982 million as of the end of Q1 2025, they have a cash runway of 1 quarter to be precise. Some revenue from their smaller horse treatment within the next 12-18 months will help to offset negative cash flow, but their need for repeated capital infusions will persist for some time. Investors must appreciate that, even if the CEO is correct in that they will not need to do any ‘major’ capital raises over the next six months, they will almost certainly have to do ‘minor’ ones, and then several in 2026. Further share dilution is basically guaranteed at this point. The question is, how much?

A silver lining of sorts is that they have no long-term debt, so it is possible they seek out a large financial institution for financing options, which may have later term dates, but for this to be an option they will have to show better evidence of future capital returns to cover the credit risk. That is why data from their phase 2 trial (SKNJCT-003), the forthcoming next-phase trial (200-400 patients), and the FDA fast-track designation for SkinJect is so important, as it provides validation of their product line and helps clarify a timeline toward commercialization. Investors both current and prospective should pay very close attention to the trial data, FDA communications, and future financial pathways carved out by Medicus to give a better sense of their potential valuation as a company.

On that note, valuing pre-revenue companies in the biotech space is notoriously difficult, as we do not know how much their total addressable market is worth, or the value of their patents or any other intangible assets, based on the fact they are not yet approved for anything and won’t be for some time. Indeed, a company’s IP is useless of no one would want to copy its technology if it does not offer a potential improvement on the current standard of care. What we do know, however, is that Medicus wants to price each treatment at $1000 for a 3-dose course. If they can reach just 10% of the annual BCC cases in the US by 2030 – those who don’t want the surgery option – that would represent 360,000 x 1,000 = $360 million, that is: a revenue rate about 7 x their current market cap. Admittedly, this is a wild guess. It is simply too early to speculate on what proportion of the US BCC market could potentially utilize their products, as we don’t have a full sense of its efficacy and safety profile, or, concomitantly, the extent that physicians will recommend it to patients. Medicus will need to create a sales team, invest more into marketing to raise awareness of the treatment, and convince the general public to opt for a potentially less efficacious product – based on the Phase 2 read out data – because it is cheaper, easier and quicker to administer. It is difficult to know what kind of uptake this would generate.

Their other treatments, acquired through the Antev deal, also have a 2027 commercial launch date in my estimation, and therefore a similarly speculative guess at future revenue generation is probably not particularly useful at this stage. Investors should keep an eye on other competitors in this space.

Conclusion

Since November, shares have been on a roller-coaster ride, with a mixture of positive trial results and news of secondary offerings impacting sentiment.

The largest daily volume (and a 30% share price decline) came when it announced a $7 million secondary stock offering at the end of May. This was its third stock offering in just six or seven months: firstly, $4 million in November 2024, and then $4.2 million in March 2025. The combined $17.2 million represents a third of its current market cap, so, as I repeat, share dilution has been significant. Based on their track record, I have no doubt the company will continue to dilute shareholders going forward, as FDA approval, let alone meaningful revenue, and let alone positive cash flow, is still at least two years away. Shareholders should be prepared for the proportionate value of their holdings to decrease as the outstanding share count increases meaningfully going forward. Insiders and institutional backers have suffered similarly. Indeed, the original backing of Melani, Bokhari and Raju, representing 20% now, just 8 months ago totalled 55% of the company. Indeed, I am encouraged by the large insider ownership. Bokhari has injected about $4 million into this business, so I have no doubt of his passion and conviction. Other insiders have also made sizeable investments. There is something comforting about knowing the CEO and other major backers have such an enormous amount riding on this. Shareholders know that management will work for them, rather than against their interests.

The rewards for the successful implementation and commercialization of Medicus’s treatments could be really significant – depending on dilution and FDA approvals, this could be a 10-bagger by 2035 – but there are major risks here. It could also go bankrupt or basically have no meaningful revenues. Everything is a possibility at this stage. The biggest risk is the potential failure to receive FDA approval for its flagship product or perhaps a poor commercial launch and uptake from physicians/patients.

For me personally, an investment here is on the ‘potentially too risky’ side, but if it drops to its recent support level (around $2.50), I would likely buy in anticipation of a positive reaction/bounce upward. My position would be very small though, and I’d write it off in my mind as essentially gambling money. While I have hedged my bets somewhat by doing quite a bit of homework on this one, there are just too many variables that I cannot fully understand or control, let alone predict here. I suspect that, going forward, the share price will remain volatile, and as we approach new trial data and other milestones over the coming months, there may be some major share price movements in either direction. Ultimately, the market will price the stock based on its future value, and only positive trial data will significantly increase this, while dilution will continue to erode it.

As we balance the two possibilities – if not likelihoods, that is: positive trial data but further dilution – I may look to build a larger position over time. I look forward to revisiting this one as an analyst perhaps at the end of the year. I’ll give this a buy rating for now but will acknowledge ahead of time that this is a long-term rather than short- or even medium-term recommendation. I believe, on balance, there is more to like about Medicus than not, and would be quite surprised if one or more FDA approvals were not granted over the next two or three years.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: First Horizon Corporation: Quality Franchise, Limited Upside
data.description:
Summary

First Horizon Corporation (NYSE:FHN) is a regional bank that combines a sticky and relationship-driven commercial lending franchise with its specialty-finance arm. Despite having best-in-class credit metrics, it is trading at a premium valuation to peers and history. I think that the company has reached fair value or is even overvalued. Thus, I would rate it a Hold given that I see limited upside due to normalized loan spreads and modest loan growth ahead.

Business Model

FHN's franchise rests on a two-pronged banking model that blends a traditional relationship-driven commercial bank with a counter-cyclical specialty finance platform. The bank is centered in Tennessee, Louisiana, Texas, and Florida. Its core business functions as a relationship-driven bank that generates ~85% of total revenue through net interest income on loans via its stable deposit base (refer to page 37 of 2025 10-Q: I derived by total interest income/(interest income + noninterest income). The relationship managers (RM) would maintain relationships with professionals within their local markets. They can be manufacturers or distributors that require term loans or working capital lines. Thus, the bank would earn from the spread above the SOFR, which is its funding cost.

For the commercial real estate side, FHN would selectively underwrite income-producing properties like retail and industrial with covenants and tighter LTVs to minimize risk. These loans are structured either "interest only" or partially amortizing. The rationale is that as borrowers want to reinvest their rental income rather than paying down their debt, FHN would accommodate that. However, that also means that the bank would take on additional duration risk, as the bulk of the principal would only be returned at the end of the loan. Thus, very careful underwriting with tight LTV ratios is paramount in issuing these loans. The bank is also well-compensated for the risk by having a wider spread for the added risks. 75% of its $62bn loan book lies in commercial lines, and the remaining is in consumer mortgages, credit cards, and other kinds.

Another important topic is its deposit franchise. It provides a low-cost, stable funding base for the bank to take underwritten risks on its loan book. FHN's ability to attract deposits even when money-market rates climb elsewhere explains that these depositors value a local banker who understands their cash flow and treasury needs. In fact, ~25% of their deposits are actually non-interest-bearing, which is a figure well above many peers. This also explains why their net interest margin of 3.4% is slightly higher than the sector median. Thus, FNH has the characteristics of a good bank via its sticky and lower-cost, stable deposit base. Its specialty finance arm acts as a counter-cyclical growth engine, generating 25% of revenue through high-turnover products like asset-based revolvers and mortgage warehouse lines. The revolvers are essentially like credit cards and are only withdrawn when needed. The mortgage warehouse line business is essentially FNH acting as the warehouse lender, as it has the balance sheet capacity to provide to independent mortgage originators at an upfront commitment fee.

Thus, both of these business arms complement each other in a virtuous cycle as the relationship-driven maintain the low-cost sticky deposits while the specialty finance arm deploys the same capital to generate high velocity, fee-rich products.

Investment Thesis

As much as I like the business and its quality, I feel that there is not much easier upside ahead. For instance, after a period of fed rate increases, its net interest margin has risen. Any further improvement depends on deposit rates falling faster than loans repricing. Also, most of the high spread commercial loans have already been repriced from the fed rate hiking peak. Thus, the easy NIM gains are behind us.

Moreover, the typical swings that would spark refinance booms or draw in asset-based revolvers are muted in today's relatively stable rate environment. Up till now, the Fed is still maintaining the rate higher for longer. The 5-year treasury yield has hovered around ~3.8% while the 3-month Treasury bill sits at ~4.3%. Thus, there is no volatility or abrupt 50bps cut that would trigger a surge in mortgage originations. Thus, in this new normal regime, originators would draw and repay warehouse lines steadily while companies tap their revolvers in-line with predictable inventory cycles. This also means that there is no opportunity for positive surprises.

Management has also reiterated low single-digit loan growth guidance, with loan growth actually declining 1.2% q/q. They have also flagged that deposit repricing costs are ticking higher by mid-single digits during Q2'25 due to greater wholesale funding, which is more expensive, and net charge-off holding at low-to-middle teens, according to recent guidance from the Wells Fargo report. This means that there is no potential catalyst for a surprise windfall for the release of the provision to boost earnings. This means that the net charge-offs were within expectations.

Therefore, all these factors are pointing to a lack of clear catalysts that could lift earnings materially or justify its rich multiples in the near term, with normalized net interest margins, specialty finance fees being predictable, while loan growth is stuck at low single digits.

Valuation

FHN is currently trading at a premium to both sector median and historical across every multiple. Its forward P/E Non-GAAP is trading at 12.8x, with a ~14% premium for both sector median and 5-year average. Its forward price/book is also trading at 1.24x, at a modest premium to the sector median and ~11% to its 5-year average. Moreover, looking at its forward revenue, growth is actually flat, and forward EBIT growth is 0.69%, lagging behind its peers.

I don't think it's premium justified based on its future growth trajectory, despite having a solid deposit franchise. Thus, this means that you're paying an extra premium for no extra growth optionality, while its multiples look like it has been "priced to perfection". Thus, its valuation is a bit too rich for my liking.

Risk

Some of the key risks that are worth monitoring. A spike in defaults may force higher provisions and dent profitability. Larger regional banks with deeper digital capabilities and lower funding costs could target the same clients and pull their customers away.

Its warehouse lines business relies on underwriting capabilities by independent mortgage originators. That is also a risk, as the due diligence is not done by them. Lastly, as the company's valuation is quite rich, any miss in estimates may lead to quick de-rating.

Conclusion

I think FHN is a decent company, especially with its strong relationship with its commercial clients, while ensuring its deposit franchise remains stable and resilient. But its net interest margins have also been mostly realized with no apparent catalyst observable in my opinion. Thus, at this valuation, I would put it a Hold till I can see there are more positive drivers ahead.

data.title: Alejandra And Kristina On Powell's Policy, Mortgage Rates And The Dollar
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast episode, Alejandra Grindal, Chief Economist at Ned Davis Research, and Kristina Hooper, Chief Market Strategist at Man Group, explain how Powell’s policy, the dollar’s decline, and stubborn mortgage rates are shaping the US economy.

Transcript

Mike Larson: The Liberation Day market massacre seems like ancient history. Worries about a slumping economy have faded. Stocks are hitting all-time highs after one of the biggest post-correction rallies on record. But the US dollar is having its worst year in more than a half century. Jay Powell is continuing to sit on his hands rather than cut interest rates at the Fed. And while the Big Beautiful Bill looks like it'll pass, one likely consequence is that US deficits and debts will continue to grow.

To help me sort out the opportunities and risks in this environment, I've brought in two of the sharpest experts in the MoneyShow roster. They've some very interesting things to say about the real risk of recession, the myths and realities of American exceptionalism, and the market outlook for the brand-new second half of 2025. They're Alejandra Grindal, Chief Economist at Ned Davis Research, and Kristina Hooper, Chief Market Strategist at Man Group. Welcome both of you to the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast.

Well, I appreciate you both joining me here for this podcast. Obviously, we've got a holiday weekend, long weekend coming up here, so hopefully everything's going well. You've got some fun plans to look forward to. But in the meantime, I guess I'll start with you, Alejandra. How would you sum up the economy in a nutshell here? I mean, are you genuinely sanguine, worried, exuberant, or is there maybe a different adjective you'd use to describe where things stand?

Alejandra Grindal: Well, as you can tell by my facial expression, sort of okay. So, first of all, as a global economist, just wanted to make the distinction with where we see global economy versus US economy. But overall, with the US being the world's largest economy and having impacts all over the world, the patterns tend to go hand-in-hand. So, I would say with respect to both the global economy and the US economy, I think, things are going sort of okay. Our outlook for the US economy and global economy is below potential growth, however, no recession. So, overall, very consistent with this sort of okay view that I mentioned.

Mike Larson: Got it. Okay. And, Kristina, I guess, from sort of a top-down perspective, I mean, how do you characterize where we stand just here in the start of the second half of the year?

Kristina Hooper: So, I would say that I'm a lot more concerned about the US economy than I am about other economies. I just believe that there are a number of different policies that could combine to create something of a perfect storm for the US. Certainly, hasn't shown up in the data yet, although we have gotten some indications in the soft data that consumer sentiment has declined. Now, we did see a recent recovery, but still rather negative sentiment, rather high inflation expectations. And I think that's only going to get worse. And I'll just give you a few of the policies that I think are really going to be problematic.

First of all, government spending cuts. I actually went back today and listened to Ross Perot, one of my personal heroes, his infomercial. You may recall, in the early 1990s, he was a debt vigilante. And he talked about how trickle-down economics doesn't work, how we need to get the deficit under control, and we're not getting our deficit under control. It's growing. And where we are cutting government spending is in the high multiplier effect areas; where we are spending to a significant extent is in lower multiplier effect areas. And so that's one, in my opinion, policy error right now.

And another one, of course, is aggressive immigration policy. If you are going in and deporting a lot of immigrants, the problem is that we're not going to necessarily have the people to fill the jobs that we need. And so, we could have a significant bout of service inflation.

Tariffs, I don't worry about so much because that tends to create a short-term impact, but what creates a longer-term sustained impact that leads to higher inflation can come from the labor market. So, I worry about that a lot. But in general, like, even student loans and the lack of forgiveness, that's going to create a lot of pressure on certain consumers.

So, all in all, to me, I worry about the US, but at the same time, I'm seeing more fiscal stimulus coming from areas like Europe and the UK. They've agreed to this NATO spending that will ratchet up, not just defense spending, but defense adjacent, which is largely infrastructure, up to 5% of GDP in the next 10 years. I think that's huge.

Mike Larson: Got it. Alejandra, let me ask you about a couple of those points there. I mean, obviously, as we're recording this, it looks like this One Big Beautiful Bill thing is likely to pass now. Thoughts on what impact that might have and/or thoughts, especially given your global focus, on where things stand in this trade and tariff saga we've been involved with?

Alejandra Grindal: Well, first of all, I wanted to mention -- did you direct it toward me? I'm sorry.

Mike Larson: Yeah, I'll start with you, Alejandra.

Alejandra Grindal: I'm just trying to make sure I heard correctly. First of all, I want to say, I agree 100% with Kristina. I know in these discussions you like to have a little bit of back and forth, but I actually agree 100%. Those are some of the risks we're looking for as well. But with respect to the Big Beautiful Bill, you're absolutely right. It looks like it's coming into fruition in some form or another.

I would say in terms of the impact on the economy and inflation, I would say near-term, both positive if you look at a lot of the details within the bill, a lot of the net stimulus is front-loaded. Incidentally while Trump was still President, makes sense, right? You want to see the benefits while you're President, and then the supposed that reductions or the cost forms are set to come actually later along the road. So, kind of to Kristina's point, it's sort of, yeah, we're getting the sugar boost now, but maybe kicking the can down the road. Again, holding all else constant, you add the upside risk coming from the immigration policies to inflation. You could also see upside risk to inflation just from the stimulative nature of the Big Beautiful Bill. So that's I would say kind of creating that anti-recession story for the United States, but obviously creating perhaps longer-term issues as a result.

In terms of the tariffs, again, like I don't have a direct rapport with the President. So, I actually don't know what the end result will be. You have a lot of different folks such as Bessent and Lutnick kind of giving different stories out there that a lot of particular deals are intact and will be finished soon, but we've heard these same stories for a week or a month now, and they haven't really come into fruition.

From at least the latest commentary I've heard, I would suspect just because of the negative market reaction we had on Liberation Day, I would suspect that if the US is working on a trade deal with some of these larger economies, it sounds like they are, supposedly, there's 10 or 12, maybe 18 discussions they've been jumping around with numbers intact, I suspect that 10% moratorium or that 10% inflation pause will probably remain until the deal stay in place. So that should at least prevent that huge knee-jerk reaction. So, I do think peak uncertainty was probably April 2, but it's not completely over just because there's really just one deal in place at this point, and the clock is running short at this time.

Mike Larson: Got it. Kristina, I wanted to ask you about your thoughts on Powell and Fed policy here. Obviously, today was another day where he kind of just said we're sitting on our hands. There's been a few cracks in the -- or fissures, I guess, of a few members of the Fed talking about maybe we're ready to cut, potentially as soon as July. What are your thoughts on what happens there? I know there's politics mixed in it, but just from kind of an economic standpoint especially?

Kristina Hooper: Yeah. Well, Chair Powell made it clear that a big obstacle to doing anything other than sitting on one's hands is to get the tariff situation resolved. At this point, there's just a really high level of uncertainty. And so, I think he views it as almost monetary policy malpractice to act now and make assumptions about what is likely to come because we really just don't know how this plays out with tariffs. So, I suspect that we're going to see the Fed sit on its hands for quite a while. We don't know, but I think chances are we'll get a tariff pause again on July 9th at least for some countries. The alternative could be a lot worse if we were to see the implementation of tariffs.

By the way, my understanding is that negotiations have gotten a little difficult at least for some countries because they want to make sure that they have an across-the-board tariff agreement as opposed to the potential for the administration to come back later and say we're going to put a higher tariff on chips or we're going to put a higher tariff on certain industries.

So, I think the Fed is being prudent to sit and wait, and I suspect that because we're probably not going to get this situation resolved right away, that'll be sitting on its hands until the fall. My expectation, my base case, is probably one cut this year unless the economy deteriorates quite significantly and the Fed is forced to act sooner, especially sooner than when we have all these tariff deals sewn up.

Mike Larson: Got it. Alejandra, what about your thoughts on the Fed here? Do you tend to agree one cut or do you see more? And, also, I'd be curious about your take. What do you think is going to happen at the longer end of the curve? I've seen more questions out there, people wondering how's housing going to perform? What's going to happen with mortgage rates? I was wondering if you had any thoughts on that.

Alejandra Grindal: Well, so first of all, with the Fed, I'm agreed so much with Kristina. I think they're definitely in wait-and-see mode, too much uncertainty. They have enough time to buy for that. Inflation is doing decently well. The economy is doing decently well. So, they can wait around. We suspect we'll get some good messages around Jackson Hole. That's kind of like that interim meeting that where we'll get information from Powell and that will let us know whether perhaps that September cut comes into fruition. If September is that first cut, I think that leaves room for a second cut. So, I would maybe different opinion with Kristina with two versus one not dramatically different.

Your second question was about the long end of the curve and longer-term interest rates. There's so many different explanations behind it, but I would like to go back and just talk about the government debt story. Like, we have charts where we just show a regression line between government debt versus long-term interest rates, and it's pretty clean relationship when you compare it among developed countries across the board, and I think that's the situation with the United States. So, if the government continues to spend in it, and it will, I mean, yes, we're going to get some tariff revenue, but it's not going to be enough to counteract the upcoming tax cuts that President Trump is intending to do and the minimum number of cuts. And then, we also have aging populations. And keep in mind that the largest expenditure of the Federal Government is social security, and no one wants to touch that. So, I think in the long run, we're still going to see higher interest rates.

Mike Larson: Got it. Kristina, I want to ask you. I mean, you just got back from a trip to the UK. We just had, what, the worst performance of the dollar index going back, I think, in the first half, since 1973. So, I'm sure that was a little more painful than it needed to be. Thoughts on why we've seen this huge move in the dollar. And also on the flip side, why we've seen such a powerful rally back from what obviously at the time looks like some pretty painful corrective activity in the market back in April?

Kristina Hooper: So, I think the move down that we've seen in the dollar to a certain extent is a retracing of strength for the US dollar for a very long time. But I also think it's a story about the US not necessarily offering the same kind of safe harbor that it had historically, especially when it comes to asset classes like US treasuries.

So, I think this is -- and, of course, we've seen a move away from the US dollar over the last couple of years as a result of US sanctions on Russia and that fear that exposure of the US dollar exposes you to more than just the currency. So, I think that this is an environment in which we're going to see investors thinking less of US treasuries as that safe haven asset class of choice, think more of gold. And in fact, in some ways, gold has replaced at least for some rules what the US dollar had been for investors.

Mike Larson: Okay. And then, equities, and, again, this is for either of you, really. Thoughts on the rebound that we've had? I mean, is this something that from an economic standpoint, from sort of market based or earning standpoint that you think has legs or you think this is a story of too far too fast?

Kristina Hooper: I think it's too far too fast, especially when we look at valuations where they are today. I mean, if we were to just take cyclically adjusted PE ratios, typically we see a significant fall from here when we reach these levels, in terms of valuations. I think the stock market is not factoring in, not accurately assessing recession risks. And I think it's because it's very hard to make assessments.

We have so much uncertainty. It's hard to model out how tariffs will impact the economy. It's hard to model out how immigration policy will impact the economy. But I would say I do think that where we are today, investors are looking at US stocks with rose-colored glasses.

Mike Larson: Got it. Alejandra, I know, again, as an economist, you kind of look at things a little bit differently. But, I mean, one thing that stood out to me from a market standpoint is just the incredible outperformance we've seen in a lot of foreign markets, vis-a-vis US markets. And I know some of it is, if you're a US investor, you get a currency kicker by investing overseas. But in general, from an economic standpoint, do you think the prospects are better in a lot of these foreign markets? Do you think some of the things like the European defense spending and so on that Kristina alluded to is going to change the composition of where the growth is?

Alejandra Grindal: I think in the intermediate term, there definitely are opportunities. Longer term, I think, that's still up in the air. Kind of going back to that question, are we seeing the end of US exceptionalism? Have those long-term growth drivers that have made the US exceptional, are they gone? But moving back to intermediate term, yeah, I do think there are some pretty good opportunities, and I think a lot of it goes back to where was growth expected to be at the beginning of the year versus where it's probably going to end up to be. And in the case of the United States, I think most people started 2024 -- sorry started -- ended 2024 and started 2025 pretty optimistic with strong growth prospects for the United States. And now, we've been revised down not just ourselves, but other folks to a well below potential rate.

Whereas if you look at Europe, you look at China, those were initially economies that were viewed as being very vulnerable to the US and a big target of President Trump. But our research found that their economies actually have relatively small exposure to the US through exports. Canada is much larger. Mexico is much larger. Vietnam. So, it actually makes sense why those economies are willing to negotiate a bit more. So, I think it's more along the lines of expectations and that surprising resiliency of some emerging markets, as well as Europe, versus what was originally expected.

Now, going to the longer-term picture, that's really what sort of up in the air. If you look at long-term growth in the United States, especially when you compare it to other developed economies, really no other economy comes close. However, if you were to ask me a year ago, is there an end to US exceptionalism from a relative growth perspective, I would say, I don't really see anything. Whereas this time around, there are elements that could reduce -- within policy that could reduce potential. So, as Kristina mentioned, immigration policy, one of the big positive drivers of the US economy has been the labor force.

And since the pre-pandemic baseline, the only growth we've seen in the labor force has been in the foreign-born population. So, if we see a reduction in both voluntary and involuntary migration, that's going to reduce potential growth. And then, from the productivity side, tariffs by definition, reduce comparative advantage and reduce productivity, which also ultimately reduces US potential growth.

Now, if I can end at least with a positive note, there's still a lot of great things in favor of the US economy. We're energy-independent, which is quite different from most other developed and even many emerging markets. And then, we're also less regulated. We could even see more deregulation with some of the policies within the tax bill or the Big Beautiful Bill, and then, we also have much more fluid labor markets. We're also the epicenter of AI. So, I still think there's still a lot of positive arguments for the US, but you could see a little bit of a leveling in the playing field, which in the long run could create just here and there more opportunities outside of the US.

Mike Larson: Sounds good. Kristina, in the time we have left, any kind of sleeper ideas or sectors or groups or asset classes we maybe haven't talked about yet, but that intrigued you? Again, it's always, we're turning the page on a new half a year, so I'm just kind of thinking if there's anything that is worth investors really paying attention to here.

Kristina Hooper: So, I don't know if I'd call them sleepers because I've been talking about them for a while now, but I think there's a heck of a lot of opportunity in European equities and to a lesser extent, UK equities. My rationale is that valuations are low. So, expectations are low, and there are those positive catalysts, most notably, of course, that increase in fiscal stimulus. So, you've got not just low valuations, high dividend yields, higher buyback yields. To me, that's a winning combination. I think that suggests there's significant potential there.

I also am very excited about China tech. I think that there hasn't been a lot of attention being given to China tech since the announcement around DeepSeek. And that was a while back now, almost six months ago. But I think there is a growing recognition that China tech in a number of industries is comparable to US tech and relatively low valuations when compared to US tech. So, I think there's a very significant opportunity there as well.

Mike Larson: Got it. Now, Alejandra, both of you are going to be joining us for the Global Investing Opportunities (and Risks), I can't forget that word, the expo we're having in a couple of weeks. When someone comes to your presentation, any idea, like thoughts on what you're going to tell them or what kind of takeaways they're going to get from that?

Alejandra Grindal: Well, so we have a lot of interesting things and maybe some resolutions and uncertainty that can happen between now and then. So, I'll be excited to talk about whether any stimulus package has gone through, any trade packages have gone through, whether any tariffs have been reinstated. But ultimately, taking this macro information will have slightly more clarity maybe at the end of this month and telling our wonderful listeners what's the best place to put asset classes in that type of environment.

Mike Larson: And I guess that would be my last question for you, Kristina, specifically, any kind of risks that we haven't mentioned or things that you think are going to be important to focus on then?

Kristina Hooper: Well, I do think US recession risk is much higher than most are factoring in. I would anticipate that the US goes into at least a modest recession within the next 12 months as a result of some of the policies I've talked about.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, listen, Alejandra, Kristina, I really do thank you both for your time and your insights here.

Originally Published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dream Unlimited: Growing Real Estate Holding Company
data.description:
I rate Dream Unlimited Corp. (DRUNF) (DRM:CA) a Hold, for investors looking for long-term buy and hold investments. The company is involved in multiple businesses, including real estate income properties, asset management, land in Western Canada, hotels, and real estate development.

Due to its size, there is limited information on the company, making it difficult to get a handle on the opportunities and risks. This makes the analysis complex in achieving solid recommendation. In these cases, it is best to hold, neither add shares nor sell them.

Overall, the company does present itself as one that is destined to grow, owning a niche that few will compete with. Judging from the activity in the stock price, it goes in boom-and-bust cycles of enthusiasm, climbing high then falling back to reality. For trading enthusiasts, this could be an opportunity right now as the stock has fallen after a parabolic rise.

The stock price reflects volatility and a level of underperformance, making a long-term investor pause before making it a larger position in their portfolio. The dividend is growing substantially, with consecutive increases and special dividends that make the stock very attractive.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Currently pays a quarterly dividend yielding 3.1% annually, with a 22.31% 5-year dividend growth rate.
  • Underperformed the S&P500, and Real Estate sector indexes since IPO in 2013
  • 80% of the value is derived from asset management, Western Canada developments and owned income properties.
  • Limited information, listed on the OTC Pink Sheets in the US and Canadian Stock market, making it less visible to US investors.

Seeking Alpha Quant does not cover the stock, nor do major Wall Street analysts.

Amounts are listed in Canadian Dollars (CAD)

Dream Unlimited: A Real Estate Holding Company

DRUNF has a market capitalization of $625.57 million, making it a micro-cap common stock. It is not a real estate investment trust (REIT).

Dream is a leading developer of office and residential assets in Toronto, owns stabilized income generating assets in both Canada and the U.S., and has an established asset management business, inclusive of $28 billion of assets under management across four Toronto Stock Exchange listed trusts and numerous partnerships.

As shown in the chart below, Dream is a holding company which includes income properties in the form of multifamilies, an asset management business with 4 public funds and 6 private ones, 8,700 acres of land in Western Canada, and development of hotels and office buildings.

Q1 Earnings Results

On May 12, 2025, the company declared Q1 earnings with the following financial highlights:

  • Asset management division added over $500 million of fee earning assets.
  • It generated revenue of $13.7 million, up 5% and a net margin of $6.1 million, up 9%.
  • Western Canada has pre-sale contracts for all land budgeted for 20025.
  • Sold 62 lots and 30 housing occupancies generating net margin of $6.3 million. $160 million of land pre-sales commitments, of which $150 million is expected to be recognized in 2025.
  • Income properties generated revenue of $11.8 million, up 15%, and net operating income of $6.4 million, up 42%, driven by higher rents and higher occupancy rates.

Overall, the first quarter results were excellent, showing growth in all major businesses. Given the size of the company, the percentage gains look very strong but can be misleading as they are off a smaller base. The takeaway is, the company is growing; there is a great deal going on with the potential to break out and become a much larger company over time.

Performance To Date

$10,000 invested over the past 10 years in DRUNF would be worth $15,870 today, which by itself is a strong gain for the time. However, over the long term, the stock is underperforming the real estate sector indexes as represented by Vanguard Real Estate Sector Index ETF (VNQ) worth $16,910, and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) worth $19,330.

In analyzing the chart below, notice the spike in price around 2022 and again at the end of 2024. For those who held the stock before the spike, it was an opportunity to trade the stock with a 100%+ gain, if one sold it. However, for those investors who bought into the enthusiasm, they were left with massive losses. As of the current period, the stock is down to reasonable levels, leaving investors to wonder if it will spike again. In my experience, if the stock were to spike and go parabolic, I would recommend selling it at 100% gain or any indications it was about to reverse.

Valuation

As exhibited in the chart above comparing to the real estate sector, DRUNF has a mixed valuation picture vs. the sector that is not completely favorable. For example, DRUNF’s dividend yield on a forward basis is 3.2%, which lags the sector median at 4.8%. Similarly, their debt-to-equity ratio of 126.7% is high vs. the sector median at 95.4%. This means that DRUNF is taking on more debt, more risk, and paying out a lower distribution.

Within the annual report deck, the company posted the chart below showing the growth of net operating income. Key takeaway is that the company is focused on growing income over time and is achieving this objective.

Since its start of distributions in 2020, Dream has consistently increased the dividend, paying a year-end special dividend, which is visualized in the chart below with the two spikes.

The table below shows how the company has had a relatively constant growth of distributions, which is very attractive, in my view. For example, one share of DRUNF in 2019 received $0.15 per share in dividends. That same share now collects $1.14 per share, a significant increase. Based on the company’s financial history, this payout looks likely to continue, in my view.

Competition

In comparison to peer real estate development companies, DRUNF stands out for being a small company (under $1 billion) with a relatively low P/E ratio at the moment.

Henderson Land Development Company Limited (OTCPK:HLDCY) currently pays a 9% dividend but has a declining distribution payout and the lowest 5-year total return of the group. I would tend to avoid this company based on these metrics.

Howard Hughes Holdings Inc. (HHH) pays no dividend with a low total return.

Forestar Group Inc. (FOR) pays no dividend with a low total return, but has a lower P/E. Its slightly larger than Dream.

AMREP Corporation (AXR) pays no dividend but has a massive 5-year total return. With a market capitalization of just over $100 million, the stock price is most likely driven by speculation.

The chart below shows total return for each peer over the past 10 years. AMREP is off the charts, showing what speculation on a small company can do to a stock. The same parabolic moves can happen to DRUNF, which reinforces a need to watch the stock closely and sell if it climbs too high too quickly.

Outlook

The future of DRUNF is very positive as the company is performing all in four of its divisions. The recent growth in AUM by the asset management group will provide fee revenue in 2025, according to the CEO. The growth in net operating income will also provide for future dividend distribution increases.

Risks To Rating

In trying to produce a rating, I was on the fence between Buy and Hold. It is possible that the stock could take off driven by positive news, but there is no guarantee. Given the size of the company, its potential for growth is definitely there. Although I listed peer companies, I didn’t see a heavy level of competition in their midst, which means Dream is operating in their own niche.

Bottom Line

I rate Dream Unlimited a Hold for investors who prefer long-time buy and hold investments. The stock may be a good candidate for trading, because it is highly volatile, making big swings up and falling back down, presumably during investor enthusiasm.

The stock currently pays a quarterly dividend of 3.1%, which is steadily growing over time. As a real estate holding company, it earns revenue from income properties, asset management, land sales in Western Canada and real estate development of hotels and office buildings.

The stock is considered a micro-cap which means it is not heavily traded or analyzed by the big Wall Street firms. Over the last 10 years, the stock has underperformed all the major indexes including the S&P500, and real estate sector indexes. However, depending on timing, an investor who bought before a parabolic move, then sold could have earned a high payout. Conversely, an investor who bought at the top of a parabolic move could be sitting on large losses.

The future of Dream is very positive. The company is performing in all four divisions and is growing net operating income.
